event: Jesus walks on the water toward the disciples' boat during a storm on the Sea of Galilee
symbols: radiant golden light surrounding Jesus — the shekinah glory of God, the divine presence shining in darkness
turbulent dark sea with crashing waves — the primordial chaos over which God alone has dominion
Jesus standing upon the waves — divine sovereignty over creation, an act Scripture attributes exclusively to YHWH
small boat tossed in the storm — the Church assailed by trials yet sustained by Christ's presence
contrast of brilliant light and surrounding darkness — the light shining in the darkness that the darkness cannot overcome
description: A luminous figure of Jesus Christ walks upon a dark, storm-tossed sea. Christ stands at upper center, surrounded by intense golden-white radiance that illuminates the waves beneath his feet. He wears flowing robes of pale rose and blue. At lower left, a small boat with huddled disciples is visible among churning dark waves. The sea is rendered in deep greens and blacks with white-capped breakers catching supernatural light. The sky is dark and stormy except where Christ's radiance breaks through. A powerful vertical axis of light descends from Christ to the water's surface.
theological_description: Aivazovsky's rendering captures one of the most theologically dense miracles in the Gospels. The event, recorded in Matthew 14, Mark 6, and John 6, is fundamentally a theophany -- a revelation of divine identity. When Jesus says 'It is I; do not be afraid,' the Greek ego eimi echoes the divine name revealed to Moses: 'I AM WHO I AM' (Exodus 3:14). The Old Testament reserves authority over the sea as a divine prerogative: God alone 'trampled the waves of the sea' (Job 9:8) and 'stilled the roaring of the seas' (Psalm 65:7). When Jesus walks on water, he performs an act attributed exclusively to YHWH, prompting the disciples' confession: 'Truly you are the Son of God' (Matthew 14:33). The storm-tossed boat has been understood since the earliest centuries as an image of the ecclesia navigating the chaos of the present age. Christ comes to them walking upon the very forces that threaten destruction -- the Gospel in visual form: salvation comes from outside, from the one who has authority over all powers of death. The dramatic light-darkness contrast embodies John 1:5: 'the light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.'

scripture_refs.reason: The direct account of Jesus walking on the sea and the disciples' confession of his divine identity
Mark's account emphasizing the theophanic nature of Jesus walking on the sea
John's account of the disciples seeing Jesus walking on the sea and being frightened
God alone trampled the waves of the sea -- walking on water as a divine prerogative Jesus exercises
I AM WHO I AM -- Jesus's ego eimi on the water echoes the divine name
He stilled the roaring of the seas -- God's exclusive authority over chaotic waters, now exercised by Christ
The light shines in the darkness and the darkness has not overcome it -- the radiance of Christ against the dark storm
They cried to the LORD in their trouble and he delivered them; he made the storm be still
